does: This paper identifies and formalizes a fundamental vulnerability in safety-aligned large language models called the Safety Paradox. The authors introduce Posterior Attack, a single-query jailbreak that exploits models by directly prompting them to generate responses their internal safety classifiers would flag as harmful. Through empirical evaluation across 30 open-source models and frontier models like GPT-4 and Claude, they demonstrate that models with superior safety-judgment capabilities are paradoxically more susceptible to this attack. The core finding is that the very mechanisms trained to recognize and refuse unsafe content create a latent capacity that attackers can exploit by inverting the model's safety awareness. The authors provide formal analysis showing that monotonic improvements in safety alignment mathematically amplify posterior vulnerability, and validate this through reinforcement learning interventions demonstrating that degrading safety judgment actually provides immunity against the attack.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature examining tensions and unintended consequences in LLM alignment, extending recent research on jailbreaking methods and safety vulnerabilities. It connects to prior work on the mechanistic interpretability of safety training, particularly research examining how models learn to recognize harmful content during alignment. The paper responds to the increasing sophistication of safety training approaches—including constitutional AI, RLHF with safety objectives, and multi-layer safety classifiers—by revealing a fundamental tradeoff rather than a limitation of any single method. It also engages with broader alignment research questioning whether standard training objectives necessarily produce robust safety properties, building on earlier observations that capability and safety improvements sometimes diverge or conflict in unexpected ways.
enables: This work opens several future directions for both offensive and defensive AI safety research. It motivates the need for new safety training paradigms that decouple safety judgment from safety execution, potentially requiring architectural innovations to prevent attackers from inverting safety evaluations. The formalization of the Safety Paradox could guide theoretical work on optimal alignment strategies that don't amplify posterior vulnerabilities. The finding suggests investigating alternative training objectives that build safety robustness without necessarily improving fine-grained harmful-content recognition. Practically, it enables development of detection methods for Posterior Attacks and could inform design of LLM deployment strategies that monitor for inverted safety evaluations. The work also points toward fundamental questions about whether current alignment approaches can achieve both strong safety judgment and safety robustness simultaneously.
